questions.question: What is the worst-case gate delay of an N-bit ripple-carry adder?
questions.explanation: In a ripple-carry adder, the carry must propagate through all N full adder stages sequentially, giving O(N) delay.

questions.question: How many select bits does an 8:1 multiplexer require?
questions.explanation: An N:1 mux needs log2(N) select bits. log2(8) = 3.

questions.question: A 3-to-8 decoder with an active-high enable produces how many outputs, and how many are active at a time when enabled?
questions.explanation: A 3-to-8 decoder has 2^3 = 8 outputs. When enabled, exactly one output is asserted (one-hot encoding).

questions.question: In a carry-lookahead adder, what do the Generate (G) and Propagate (P) signals represent?
options: G = a XOR b, P = a AND b
G = a AND b, P = a XOR b
G = a OR b, P = a XOR b
G = a XOR b, P = a OR b
questions.explanation: Generate G_i = a_i AND b_i means this stage produces a carry regardless of the incoming carry. Propagate P_i = a_i XOR b_i means an incoming carry passes through. These signals let all carries be computed in parallel.

questions.question: What is the difference between a half adder and a full adder?
options: A half adder is twice as fast
A full adder accepts a carry-in bit in addition to two operand bits
A half adder can only add 4-bit numbers
A full adder uses twice as many transistors but produces the same output
questions.explanation: A half adder adds two single bits, producing a sum and carry. A full adder adds three bits: two operands plus a carry-in from a previous stage. Full adders are chained together to build multi-bit adders like ripple-carry and carry-lookahead adders.
